piec
  • Anisotropic Magnetoresistance (AMR)
  • View page source

Anisotropic Magnetoresistance (AMR)

Anisotropic Magnetoresistance (AMR) is a magnetotransport effect in ferromagnetic materials where the electrical resistance depends on the angle between the current direction and the magnetization orientation.

Background

The AMR effect arises from spin-orbit coupling. When an external magnetic field saturates the magnetization, conduction electron scattering depends on the relative orientation of spin and momentum. This produces a resistance that varies as:

\[R(\theta) = R_\perp + (R_\parallel - R_\perp) \cos^2\theta\]

where θ is the angle between the current and magnetization directions. An AMR measurement sweeps θ from 0° to 360° by physically rotating the sample in a constant saturating field, recording resistance (or lock-in voltage) at each angle.

Required instruments

  • Lock-in amplifier — measures the voltage response with high sensitivity (e.g., SR830)

  • DMM — optional DC resistance measurement (e.g., Keithley193A)

  • DC calibrator — provides the control voltage for the electromagnet power supply and the AC current source for the lock-in (e.g., EDC522)

  • Stepper motor controller — rotates the sample stage (e.g., Arduino with stepper driver)

Example

from piec.drivers.sr830 import SR830
from piec.drivers.keithley193a import Keithley193A
from piec.drivers.edc522 import EDC522
from piec.drivers.arduino import Arduino
from piec.measurement.magneto_transport import AMR

lockin     = SR830('GPIB0::8::INSTR')
dmm        = Keithley193A('GPIB0::7::INSTR')
calibrator = EDC522('GPIB0::5::INSTR')
arduino    = Arduino('COM3')

experiment = AMR(
    dmm=dmm,
    calibrator=calibrator,
    arduino=arduino,
    lockin=lockin,
    field=1000.0,            # Applied field in Oersted
    angle_step=15,           # Step size in degrees
    total_angle=360,         # Total rotation in degrees
    amplitude=1.0,           # Lock-in reference amplitude (V)
    frequency=13.7,          # Lock-in reference frequency (Hz)
    measure_time=60,         # Averaging time per point (s)
    sensitivity='50uV/nA',
    save_dir='./data',
    voltage_callibration=10000,  # Oe/V for the field control calibration
)

experiment.run_experiment()

Key parameters

  • field (float) — magnetic field strength in Oersted applied during rotation.

  • angle_step (float) — angular increment in degrees per measurement point.

  • total_angle (float) — total angular range of the sweep in degrees.

  • amplitude, frequency — lock-in reference signal parameters.

  • measure_time (float) — averaging duration in seconds at each angular step.

  • voltage_callibration (float) — conversion factor from control voltage to field (Oe/V).

Output

After run_experiment() completes, the following are saved to save_dir:

  • Raw data file — angle, lock-in X, lock-in Y (and optionally DMM resistance) at each angular step.

  • R(θ) plot — resistance (or lock-in magnitude) as a function of rotation angle, showing the characteristic cos²(θ) AMR signature.

API reference

  • MagnetoTransport

  • AMR
